does: This paper introduces JamSet and JamBench, the first large-scale dataset and benchmark for evaluating AI systems on project-level code engineering within professional game engines. The authors mine Game Jam competitions to extract 8,133 verified Godot engine projects from over 240,000 repositories, creating a deterministic evaluation pipeline that checks file integrity and runtime behavior. They curate 300 manually verified projects into JamBench and define two core tasks: theme-driven game code generation and code completion. The evaluation combines compilation success rates, structural metrics, and runtime behavior collection, enabling systematic assessment of AI models on realistic, large-scale game development projects with measurable outcomes beyond toy examples.
connects: This work extends recent progress in AI-driven game development by moving beyond isolated asset generation and web-based coding tasks toward realistic, project-scale engineering challenges. It builds on growing interest in using open-source repositories as training data for code models, similar to efforts in general software engineering datasets like CodeSearchNet and The Stack. The paper responds to limitations in existing game AI benchmarks which focus on gameplay design or individual asset creation rather than cohesive framework development. By leveraging Game Jam events as data sources, it parallels successful uses of competition-driven datasets in other domains and complements prior work on deterministic testing and evaluation of code generation systems.
enables: This benchmark enables future research in several directions: training and evaluating code generation models specifically for game development contexts where framework decisions constrain downstream creativity, studying how AI systems handle project-scale architectural decisions and dependency management, and developing specialized techniques for domain-specific code completion. The deterministic verification pipeline could be adapted to other professional engines and software domains, establishing new standards for evaluating code generation beyond compilation metrics. The dataset may support research on game AI that reasons about thematic coherence and gameplay mechanics as code-level constraints, and enable better understanding of how AI handles the coordination of multiple files and systems typical of real game development.
